index.name: Nifty IT Index
index.description: Nifty IT tracks 10 companies generating $250B+ annual exports — 15% of India's total exports. Tier-1 majors: TCS (28.9%), Infosys (24.6%), HCL Tech (14.8%), Wipro (12.3%). India's IT sector accounts for 5.4M jobs, ~5% of India's GDP. Structural advantages: English-language competency, timezone arbitrage (US daytime = India nighttime), cost advantage (developer salary ₹40L vs $150K US), and global onsite-offshore model. Currency play: 60%+ revenues earned in USD; when rupee weakens 10%, reported rupee revenues rise equivalently. Bull cycle driver: Generative AI adoption in enterprise — IT spending growth accelerating from 6% to 12%+ in AI-driven consulting, platform modernization, and custom software development. Bear cycle driver: US tech spending contraction — most revenue from BFSI, telecom, and hi-tech verticals which curtail IT budgets during recessions. Leverage to market multiples: during risk-on sentiment, IT trades at 25-30x PE; during risk-off, contracts to 15-18x PE. Margin compression risk: wage inflation in India (30% CAGR for fresher salaries from 2010-2023) eroding profitability despite revenue growth.

index.name: Nifty IT Index
index.description: Nippon India ETF IT tracks the Nifty IT Index  India's 10 largest technology services companies. India's IT sector is the world's largest IT services outsourcing ecosystem, with $254B in FY25 revenues and targeting $500B by 2030. The sector benefits from structural demand: AI transformation projects (cloud migration, GenAI implementation, enterprise automation) require massive workforce that only India can supply competitively. Wage arbitrage persists  Indian engineers cost 20-25% of US equivalents for same productivity. Key investment thesis: every $1 of AI infrastructure investment by Big Tech requires $10-15 of AI implementation services  the primary beneficiary is Indian IT services. Cyclical risk: US/Europe IT spending cuts during recession compress margins. However, discretionary project deferral is offset by non-discretionary managed services contracts (40-60% of revenues) providing floor revenues.
